title: Eval Loop Strategy
summary: Notes comparing weekly human review, automated scoring, holdout cases, and release guardrails.

sources.title: Quality review memo
sources.body:
Teams need smaller eval batches with visible failure clusters before expanding model coverage. The last broad run increased the number of cases scored, but reviewers could not tell which failures were repeated patterns and which were one-off mistakes.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the eval strategy case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Smaller eval batches make failure clusters easier to inspect. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Citation support must remain an explicit shipping gate when coverage improves.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not recommend expansion based only on coverage gains. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Release guardrail notes
sources.body:
Coverage can improve while grounding regresses, so citation support needs an explicit shipping gate. The suggested threshold is not a leaderboard target; it is a release block when candidate outputs make unsupported claims.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the eval strategy case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Citation support must remain an explicit shipping gate when coverage improves. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Trace depth is useful but retry behavior needs a cost guardrail.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not ignore the 1.34x retry-cost signal. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Cost review
sources.body:
Long traces help debug failures, but unchecked retries created a 1.34x cost increase in the last dry run. The finance reviewer accepted trace retention only if retries are capped and summarized in the run manifest.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the eval strategy case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Trace depth is useful but retry behavior needs a cost guardrail. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Holdout labels should not be exposed during prompt iteration.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not expose holdout tuning labels as part of the Genie product surface. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Holdout protocol
sources.body:
Holdout cases should appear in dashboards as summaries and aggregate scores, but their tuning labels should stay out of prompt-iteration workflows. Reviewers want confidence that prompt changes are not simply memorizing visible examples.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the eval strategy case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Holdout labels should not be exposed during prompt iteration. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Smaller eval batches make failure clusters easier to inspect.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not recommend expansion based only on coverage gains. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Smaller eval batches make failure clusters easier to inspect. Citation support must remain an explicit shipping gate when coverage improves. Trace depth is useful but retry behavior needs a cost guardrail. Holdout labels should not be exposed during prompt iteration.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are citation-grounding, cost-guardrail, holdout-boundary. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not recommend expansion based only on coverage gains. Do not ignore the 1.34x retry-cost signal. Do not expose holdout tuning labels as part of the Genie product surface.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Recommend whether to expand automated evals, balancing coverage, citation support, trace utility, and cost. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
